sources_note: Growing guidance on this page draws on Australian berry-industry and horticultural sources, the rare-fruit and horticultural society archives, university berry research, and state agriculture and biosecurity sources.

core.intro: Across Australia the one thing every raspberry wants is the same: cool conditions and steady moisture. They are a cool-climate cane fruit, happiest with a genuinely cold winter, a mild summer and a damp, free-draining, slightly acidic soil. Get the type right for your climate and keep the roots cool and moist, and raspberries are generous, if short-lived, croppers. The state sections cover the climate, regions, harvest timing and pests where you are; the notes below apply anywhere in the country.

sections.heading: Summer or autumn? Choose the type first
sections.body: The first decision is not the variety but the type, because it sets when you pick and how you prune. Summer-fruiting (floricane) raspberries such as Tulameen, Chilcotin, Chilliwack and the very early Sandford fruit in early to midsummer on canes that grew the year before. Autumn-fruiting (primocane or everbearing) raspberries such as Heritage, Autumn Bliss and Coho fruit in late summer and autumn on canes that grew the same season, and golden (yellow) raspberries are usually autumn types too. Summer types give one big concentrated crop and the largest, best-flavoured berries (Tulameen is the benchmark); autumn types are simpler to prune and stretch the picking later. Many gardeners grow one of each for a long season. The in-stock table above shows which named varieties are available right now.

sections.heading: Pollination: one plant will crop
sections.body: Raspberries are self-fertile, so a single plant sets fruit on its own and you do not need a second variety as a pollinator. They are still insect-pollinated, and bees do the work, so a well-visited patch sets fuller berries. Each raspberry is a cluster of many tiny segments (drupelets), and when only some of them are pollinated you get the small, lopsided, crumbly berries that fall apart in your fingers, so keep bees happy and avoid spraying anything while the canes are in flower.

sections.heading: Planting and soil
sections.body: Raspberries want full sun (six to eight hours, with a little afternoon shade only in the hottest districts) and a moisture-retentive but free-draining, slightly acidic soil of about pH 5.5 to 6.5, rich in organic matter. They are shallow-rooted and hate two things above all, drying out and sitting wet, so dig in plenty of well-rotted manure and compost before planting, and on heavy clay either fork in gypsum and build the bed up or plant in a raised bed, because waterlogged ground brings on Phytophthora root rot, the disease most likely to kill garden raspberries outright. Give the canes a simple post-and-wire trellis to lean on, space them along the row, and mulch thickly with straw, sugar cane or bark to keep the roots cool and damp and to hold the weeds down (see our guide on when to plant). Bare-rooted canes go in over the dormant winter months.

sections.heading: Water and feeding
sections.body: Those shallow roots mean raspberries need steady, even moisture, never a flood and never a drought. The make-or-break window runs from flowering through to the fruit filling and ripening, because the berries are mostly water and a dry spell then leaves you with small, seedy, crumbly fruit. As a rule of thumb the plants want roughly 25 to 40 mm of water a week through the growing season, more in hot weather, and they are best watered at the base by drip or soaker hose rather than overhead, since wet foliage and fruit invite grey mould and leaf disease. Keep a thick mulch on at all times to hold that moisture and cool the soil, and make sure the bed drains freely, because even a few days of waterlogging can kill the roots. Feed them, but feed them lightly: too much nitrogen drives soft, leafy, disease-prone canes at the expense of fruit, and on research crops over-fed canes grow tall, thin and weak. A complete or balanced fertiliser, or organic blood and bone with well-rotted manure, worked in as growth starts in early spring (with an optional second feed as the fruit sets) suits them, and a dressing of sulphate of potash supports fruiting; ease off feeding by late summer so the canes harden before winter. The point is little and often: commercial Pacific North-West guides apply only a modest, split dose of nitrogen across the season (in the order of 55 to 90 kilograms per hectare), which for a backyard row means a light hand, not one heavy feed.

sections.heading: Pruning: it depends on the type
sections.body: Pruning raspberries is simple once you know your type, and getting it wrong costs you the crop. Summer-fruiting (floricane) types fruit on two-year-old canes, so once they finish in summer you cut the canes that just fruited right down to the ground and keep the strong new green canes, tying them to the wire for next year. Autumn-fruiting (primocane) types fruit on the current season's canes, so the easiest method is to cut every cane to the ground in winter while the plant is dormant, after which it throws up fresh canes that fruit the following autumn. (You can instead leave an autumn type's canes standing to steal an earlier, lighter summer crop the next year, called double-cropping, but mowing the lot down gives one bigger, cleaner crop and is far less fiddly.) Never cut a summer type to the ground in winter or you remove the very canes that were about to fruit.

sections.heading: Harvest
sections.body: Raspberries ripen from about November in the warmest districts through to autumn, with summer types picking in early to midsummer and autumn types from late summer on. A berry is ready when it has full colour and slips off its little white core (the plug, or torus) with the gentlest tug, and that hollow centre is what tells a raspberry from a blackberry, which keeps its core. They do not ripen any further once picked, and they bruise and mould within a day or two, so pick into shallow trays every couple of days in the cool of the morning and eat, freeze or cook them quickly. An autumn type can give a small crop in its first year and a summer type from its second, with both hitting full stride by about the third.

sections.heading: Buying tips: start clean
sections.body: Raspberries are prone to a slow decline from aphid-spread viruses, which show as mottled leaves, stunted plants and crumbly fruit, and which is why a patch is often worth replacing after about ten years. The single best defence is to start with certified virus-free, named stock from a nursery rather than digging up suckers from a friend's old (and possibly infected) patch. Buy dormant bare-rooted canes in winter or potted plants, check the label for whether it is a summer or autumn type so you know how to prune it, and match the variety to your climate, because raspberries are a cool-climate plant at heart. The in-stock table above shows the named varieties available right now and what they cost.

faqs.q: Can I grow raspberries in a pot or a small garden?
faqs.a: Yes. Raspberries grow well in a large tub at least 40 cm across and deep, which suits a courtyard or a small garden and lets you stand them somewhere cooler over summer. An autumn-fruiting type is the easiest in a container because you simply trim it back hard each winter. Use a quality potting mix, water often since pots dry out fast, feed through the warmer months, and stand the pot in full sun with netting against birds.

faqs.q: Will raspberries take over the garden?
faqs.a: They can wander if you let them. Raspberries spread by underground runners and throw up new canes (suckers) a surprising distance from the parent, so over a few years one plant becomes a thicket. Keep them to a tidy strip by slicing off or mowing the suckers that stray outside the row, sinking a physical root barrier around the bed, or growing them in a large pot where the roots cannot escape.

faqs.q: Do raspberries really need a cold winter?
faqs.a: Mostly, yes. The common raspberry is a cool-climate plant that wants a genuinely cold winter and a mild summer, and in warm, humid or hot-summer areas it sulks, crops poorly and is short-lived. If your winters are mild, either grow it only in your coolest spot with the roots well shaded and mulched, or look to a warm-climate native raspberry instead, which is a different plant altogether.

faqs.q: How many raspberry plants do I need for a worthwhile crop?
faqs.a: Fewer than you might think, because each plant multiplies into a row of canes. Half a dozen canes of one type, grown along a couple of metres of trellis, will keep a household in fresh berries through the season, and ten or so will give you enough to freeze and make jam. Grow a summer type and an autumn type side by side and you stretch that picking over many more weeks.

faqs.q: Can I grow raspberries from seed or from supermarket berries?
faqs.a: It is not worth it. Raspberry seed is slow and unreliable and the seedlings will not come true to the named variety, and the berries you buy in punnets are not a practical source of plants. Start instead from certified canes or potted plants of a named variety, which crop far sooner, true to type, and let you choose a summer or autumn type to suit how you want to pick and prune.

WA.intro: Growing raspberries in Western Australia. Raspberries are a cool-climate cane fruit, so in WA they belong in the cool, higher-rainfall south, not on the warm Perth coastal plain. Get them into the right district and keep the roots cool and damp, and the southern forests and hills grow them well.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: The warm Perth plain is marginal for raspberries: they want real winter chill and resent hot, dry summers and warm nights, and the alkaline coastal sands are the wrong soil for them. The crop comes good in the cooler, wetter, higher south, where summers are milder. Driscoll's, the country's largest berry grower, runs part of its summer raspberry production in the south-west of Western Australia, which tells you where the climate suits them. On the Perth plain itself, treat raspberries as a pot or raised-bed plant in the coolest, root-shaded spot you have.

sections.heading: Where raspberries grow in WA
sections.body: The cool districts are the ones to look to. Donnybrook, the orchard country a couple of hours south of Perth, the Great Southern around Albany, Denmark, Mount Barker and the Porongurup, and the high, high-rainfall Perth Hills around Bickley and Pickering Brook all grow berries and cool-climate fruit. As a rule, the further south and the higher you are, the easier raspberries become.

sections.heading: Harvest window in WA
sections.body: Western Australia's southern raspberries crop through the warmer months, with summer-fruiting types picking from around December and autumn-fruiting types carrying the season on into autumn. The milder the summer, the slower and sweeter the ripening, which is why the deep south does best.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in WA
sections.body: WA's drier summers help keep the fruit and leaf diseases of the humid east in check, though grey mould still appears in wet spells, so water at the base and keep the canes open. The fruit fly here is Mediterranean fruit fly, which is endemic across the south-west (its main belt runs from Perth down through Bunbury, Donnybrook and Manjimup), not Queensland fruit fly, which WA keeps out and eradicates whenever it is found. Net against birds, which are the number one raspberry pest everywhere.

sections.heading: Buying and shipping to WA
sections.body: WA's quarantine controls what live plant material can cross the border, and high-risk plants may not be allowed in or must meet strict conditions, so many eastern nurseries will not post live raspberry canes to WA. The in-stock table above is filtered to sellers that can ship here, and locally grown, certified stock is the simplest and safest way to start a patch.

faqs.q: Which raspberry varieties suit Perth and the south-west?
faqs.a: On the warm coastal plain, lean on autumn-fruiting types grown in a pot or raised bed of cool, acidic mix with the roots shaded, and expect a modest crop. In the cool south you can grow the full range, including the premium summer types like Tulameen, which reward the extra chill with their best size and flavour.

faqs.q: Can I bring raspberry canes into WA from the eastern states myself?
faqs.a: Usually not freely. WA quarantine restricts live plants coming in, so canes posted or carried from interstate can be refused or need to meet strict conditions, and they must be free of soil. The easy path is to buy Western Australian grown, certified virus-free stock, which sidesteps the border rules and the risk of importing disease.

QLD.intro: Growing raspberries in Queensland. Most of the state is too warm and humid for the common raspberry, which is a cool-climate plant, so it is a crop for the high, cold Granite Belt. Queensland does, though, have a warm-climate raspberry all its own: the native Atherton raspberry.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Common raspberries (Rubus idaeus) need a cold winter and a mild summer, which rules out the warm, humid Queensland coast, the subtropics and the tropics, where they get too little chill and too much heat and humidity. The one part of the state that genuinely suits them is the high, cool Granite Belt. Everywhere warmer, the home-grown answer is the native Atherton raspberry, a different species that actually likes the warmth.

sections.heading: Where raspberries grow in QLD
sections.body: The Granite Belt around Stanthorpe, at 800 to 1000 metres with frosty winters and the highest chill in Queensland, is the state's cool-climate fruit district and the one place common raspberries are grown commercially, under polytunnels, across a long season that runs from about November to June. Away from that highland pocket, the climate works against the European raspberry.

sections.heading: The Atherton raspberry, a native for warm areas
sections.body: Queensland is the stronghold for Australia's native Rubus, and the best known is the Atherton raspberry (Rubus probus), a rainforest species found widely through Queensland from the Helidon Hills to Cooktown. It is a vigorous, scrambling, very prickly cane rather than a tidy compact bush, so give it room and gloves, but it thrives in warm, humid country where the common raspberry will not, fruiting through summer and autumn and sometimes much of the year, with a sharp, strong raspberry flavour. The native R. rosifolius is another widespread red-fruited species. These are the realistic raspberries for the warm Queensland lowlands.

sections.heading: Harvest window in QLD
sections.body: Granite Belt raspberries are picked across a long polytunnel season from about November to June, while the native Atherton raspberry fruits through the warm months and can crop on and off for much of the year in the right spot, a useful difference for a warm-climate garden.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in QLD
sections.body: Queensland fruit fly is the fruit fly here, a major pest across eastern Australia that is most active in warm, humid weather, so soft ripe berries need protecting where it is bad. Humidity also lifts grey mould and leaf disease, so keep the canes open and airy and water at the base rather than over the fruit, and net against birds.

faqs.q: Can I grow ordinary raspberries in Brisbane or the warm Queensland lowlands?
faqs.a: Not really. Brisbane and the warm coast are too mild in winter and too humid in summer for the common raspberry to crop well, so it tends to grow leaf, sulk and fade. The better answer in warm country is the native Atherton raspberry, which is built for it, or a few autumn-type canes in a pot in deep shade as a novelty rather than a real crop.

faqs.q: Is the native Atherton raspberry sold as a plant?
faqs.a: Yes. Native-plant and bush-tucker nurseries sell the Atherton raspberry (Rubus probus), and it is one of the few raspberries that actually suits a warm, humid Queensland garden. Just plan for a vigorous, prickly scrambler that wants space and a trellis or fence to clamber on, not a neat row of canes.

NSW.intro: Growing raspberries in New South Wales. Raspberries are a cool-climate cane fruit that needs real winter chill, so in NSW they belong on the cool tablelands and in the highlands, not on the warm coast or the hot inland plains.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: The New South Wales coast and the hot western inland are too warm for raspberries to get the winter chill and the cool summers they need, but the state's cool, elevated districts suit them well. The higher and colder the site, the better and longer they crop, which is why every established NSW raspberry district is up on the ranges.

sections.heading: Where raspberries grow in NSW
sections.body: The Southern Highlands around Mittagong, Bowral and Robertson are an established cool-climate berry district, with raspberry farms growing fruit for picking through summer. Other cool districts that suit them are the Central Tablelands around Orange and Oberon, the New England and Northern Tablelands around Armidale, and the Blue Mountains, all cold enough in winter to crop raspberries well.

sections.heading: Harvest window in NSW
sections.body: On the cool tablelands raspberries are picked through summer and into early autumn, roughly December to March, later than the warm-climate berry crops of the far north and stretched out further still in the coldest, highest gardens.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in NSW
sections.body: Queensland fruit fly is established through eastern New South Wales and will sting soft ripe fruit, so net or bait for it where it is active, and net against birds everywhere. The wetter, more humid ranges and the coast lift grey mould pressure on the berries, so keep the canes open and airy and water at the base, not over the fruit.

faqs.q: Can I grow raspberries on the New South Wales coast or in Sydney?
faqs.a: It is hard work. The coast and the Sydney basin are warm and humid with little winter chill, so raspberries crop lightly and tend to be short-lived there. If you want to try, grow an autumn-fruiting type in the coolest, most shaded-root corner you have, or in a pot you can move, and treat a modest pick as a win.

faqs.q: Which raspberry type is best for a cold tableland garden?
faqs.a: Both types thrive on the chill, so the choice is about how you want to pick. Summer types like Tulameen and Chilcotin repay the cold with big, premium early-to-midsummer berries, while autumn types carry the season on later. Many tableland gardeners plant one of each to pick from early summer right through into autumn.

VIC.intro: Growing raspberries in Victoria. Victoria is the mainland's raspberry heartland: its cold winters and mild summers are close to ideal, and the hills east of Melbourne have grown berries for generations.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Victoria gives raspberries exactly what they want, a properly cold winter to satisfy their chill and a cool to mild summer that ripens the fruit slowly without cooking the plant. It is the most reliable mainland state for them, and even the cool, wet districts that struggle with other fruit suit raspberries down to the ground.

sections.heading: Where raspberries grow in VIC
sections.body: The Dandenong Ranges and the Yarra Valley east of Melbourne, around Silvan, Wandin, Monbulk and Coldstream, are the traditional heart of mainland raspberry growing and produce the bulk of the mainland crop, with more berries through Gippsland and the cool hill country. Pick-your-own raspberry farms have run in these hills for generations.

sections.heading: Harvest window in VIC
sections.body: Victoria's cool season ripens raspberries late and over a long stretch, with picking running from about November right through to autumn, the longest mainland season. The cooler the district, the later and more drawn-out the pick.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in VIC
sections.body: Birds are the main pest, so net before the fruit colours. Queensland fruit fly has become established in parts of Victoria, mainly the northern Goulburn and Murray districts, and is managed under area-wide programs, so net or bait where it occurs. Cool, wet springs and autumns favour grey mould on the fruit, and yellow rust may spot the leaves in damp summers though it rarely cuts the crop, so keep the canes open and water at the base.

faqs.q: Which raspberries grow best around Melbourne?
faqs.a: Melbourne's cool climate grows the whole range, so you can take your pick. The premium summer types such as Tulameen, and reliable autumn types such as Heritage and Autumn Bliss, all crop well, and the cooler hill suburbs and the ranges are about as good as mainland Australia gets for raspberries.

faqs.q: Do I need to protect raspberries from frost in Victoria?
faqs.a: Hardly at all. Dormant raspberry canes are very cold-hardy and a proper cold winter is a help, not a threat, so frost is rarely the problem here. The real jobs in Victoria are netting the birds and keeping the ripe fruit dry in damp weather; only a hard, late spring frost on the open flowers of the earliest types is worth a second thought.
